sentences.text: This was how I escaped getting out of the ghetto.
I would like to tell you how I escaped smuggling into the ghetto dynamite.
This was a different time.
It was on the point, the smuggling point, of [POLISH].
And over there, a group of Polish smugglers smuggled in food into the ghetto and of course, Poles.
This was their way of making a living for some smugglers, a very dangerous one because the ghetto wall was always patrolled by German, by Polish police.
But in this particular place, it was the Jewish police and the Polish police was bribed.
And it was a ladder put at the wall of one side, on the Polish side, of the ghetto wall.
And I was at that time already told by phone to my group, [INAUDIBLE], where I am going to smuggle in.
And these were packages of five kilo dynamite put in greasy paper to look like I am smuggling butter.
Usually, the smugglers paid a certain amount of money to the foreman and he was staying near the ladder.
And they gave him the money, went up the ladder on the other side, and jumped on the other side of the wall into the ghetto.
And the smuggle was going on and that was mine next and I gave also the money to the foreman and I went up with my package.
It was not suspicious for them.
And while I was on top of the wall, shooting started.
And I was on top and shooting started to come closer.
The foreman snatched away the ladder and I was not able not to go back to the Polish side and I didn't see anybody on the Jewish side.
And I was afraid to jump, not knowing too much about dynamite, that maybe this could explode.
So I was on top and the shooting came closer and I was sure that I am done.
But suddenly, the two people who were waiting for me saw me there on top and they made a human ladder from the ghetto side and brought me down.
And we ran away just in time.
So this was another time of smuggling.
A third time I remember also while I smuggled in dynamite it was in a factory, [INAUDIBLE] factory, on [POLISH] Street, which one part of the factory was on the Polish side where the door was.
The other side, where the windows were, were on the Jewish, in the ghetto.
And they told me that this is going to be the best way how to smuggle in.
And of course, I came in and the guide, which is guarding, was there and he let me in.
But I was having this vis-a-vis opposite the factory is the Jewish cemetery.
So it was a German guarding [INAUDIBLE] cemetery.
But I got in into the factory and he told me-- he took me to the window there and told me, OK, give me your package.
He didn't know what I'm smuggling in, the Polish guard.
But the package didn't go through the window.
I have to repack this.
And I start repack it together with him and he was terribly nervous.
I was nervous, too.
And the people on the other side from the ghetto were calling, faster, faster, faster.
And finally, I made it in smaller packages and we pushed it through.
And he asked me when I gave him the vodka, the [POLISH] at that time and some money, he asked me, what did you have there?
And I told him, paint.
He said, really, paint only?
I said, yes, only paint.
And I went away.
This was one time when I was just scared.
So there were all kinds of situations where you have to act and to be alert and to have luck.
Otherwise, many of my co-workers, who also were doing the same kind of work, they fell, they were killed.
No.
They were doing strictly to make a living, for money.
These were not underground.
Maybe one, I don't know.
Because those who did it in the underground were very careful to share what they are doing with anybody else.
To bribe them?
Then I have to reveal what I have.
Sometimes you can bribe somebody, but usually you will never show because they can also denounce you.
And many, as I said, were denounced and killed because of Poles who did give up.
At that time when I was preparing and doing my work on the Polish side, none of my family remained.
My mother and sister and brother were deported already.
And before deportation, they knew that I am involved.
But at that time, I was still in the ghetto so they didn't know exactly what I was doing.
But they knew that I was involved in illegal work.
At that time when they were deported, it was blocked-- I see it before my eyes.
It was on [?

Leshner ?] Street and it was about-- after 4 o'clock-- and a Jewish policeman came to his family telling them that they heard on the Jewish police station that this street is going to be blocked and deported.
And we waited all day long together with the family.
But I was in a separate place, and by 5:00 when everybody gave up-- nothing took place, nothing happened.
Mother and my brother went up to prepare something to eat-- --and at that moment when they went up, the street was blocked and deportations started, selections started.
And I couldn't see them because the street was full of people.
I couldn't see not my brother, I couldn't go up to them anymore.
And I at that time was having just a piece of paper to show that I am working in a German workshop, [INAUDIBLE] workshops.
And when I gave it to the German, he looked at me.
I showed him the paper, he looked again at me, and sent me to the right.
I was young at that time.
When I was on the right side for the people-- I didn't know even if this is the right, if it is-- but it was the right side.
The selection was still going on.
I didn't see my brother and I didn't see my mother.
My sister was with her workshop in the kitchen, with the staff.
So I was looking for them and suddenly, I heard when the van was passing by calling my name.
I want to go to where the voice is coming from, but I was separated.
They cut me off, they didn't let me go to move away.
And they were taken away.
I didn't see them.
I just-- in my mind I just heard the voice.
Later on, I wanted to go together to go to the so-called [POLISH], the deportation place, but I couldn't go through.
They didn't let me.
And this is how I was separated and they were deported.
Yes, I knew.
All the Jews from Warsaw were deported to Treblinka, to the gas chambers.
Now when I go to Poland once a year, my first walk is to Treblinka.
This is the cemetery.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My sister was also deported with her group of people from the staff another day.
Yes.
To my mind at that time, when the whole family was gone, was deep, deep depression.
I was days crying.
The only thing which kept me was the few friends which I had in the shop, and then it was the assignment which I got.
And in a way, this was something to hold on.
It was not a question to be afraid, it was a question to do something.
And I was absolutely-- --done and committed to every little thing which I have to do.
And this was everything, really, which was worth living for.
Fear?
I assumed that I had fear, but I didn't think about fear.
Very often, after an assignment when-- when I have to do something, I was doing it.
When I saw that it's danger, I had fear.
But I didn't live with the fear.
At night sometimes, when in my mind I went back, I sometimes-- I got terrible fear.
Oh, it could have been like this or that.
But when things were going on, you do what you think you have to.
I knew my husband when I was on the Polish side.
I met him a few times, we got together.
I even went up to the hiding place where his parents were there.
And it is-- what it means Jewish tradition.
While coming to the hiding place-- and there were times when the Poles have, curfew too, during the occupation.
And Ben was in my house and couldn't go back in time to their house, sometimes to their hiding place, which was far away.
And they knew that he remained with me.
So one time I came to the hiding place there on this a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the mother took out a ring and she said to me, I know where Ben is, the time he's with you.
I would like you to have the ring.
This is my ring.
It was my engagement ring.
And she gave me the ring as an engagement ring.
In other words, as an acceptance of the situation.
And his father was very religious, the mother was also a religious person.
But this was the way of understanding at this particular time.
Yes, I still have the ring.
Yes, it was already a relationship for me from a girl with a boy.
Yes, it was still two young people in a relationship in such circumstances.
As I said, we knew that every moment is in danger.
But nevertheless, there are feelings and we had the relationship.
It was the youth, it was the belief.
And probably we believed that we will see the end of it.
Otherwise-- --I cannot explain it in a different way.
For me, it was also very important that I knew that I have somebody so close who cares, who if I will not exist it is a person who will find out maybe, who will look for me.
And this was very, very much for me to have the feeling that somebody loves you and cares for you and is part of you.
And it was natural, this kind of relationship, at that time for young people, although danger was surrounding us.
It was-- this was the life, full of danger.

Well, when I went to visit his parents it was times when I got-- the underground has sometimes-- --a small amount of money which came from-- from the-- from England, from the Polish government in exile.

We had representatives Otto [POLISH] and [POLISH] and sometimes American labor unions and organizations, Jewish Labor Committee sent through illegal ways money to England and from there, Polish parachute, like [POLISH], which he now lives in Washington, DC, an older gentleman.
He was a Polish officer in the Polish underground which used to parachute to Poland with all kinds of money for the Polish underground and bring a small amount also for the Jewish underground.
At that time, we had a number of Jews we knew hiding among Poles.
And we used to pay the Poles a small amount of money to be able to-- the Jews to keep them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And such a small amount of money, I was the one very often going to the Polish places where Jews were hiding and bringing.
I went also to the place where my husband's parents were and the people there didn't know that I am Jewish.
They knew that I am in the Polish underground.
And this was where I used to come.
And-- --I recall that Friday the mother used to have candles, lighted candles.
They-- religious little traditional things they use in the hiding to go on.
My husband has a little sister who lives now in Israel, a girl of 10 years, she was with them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very difficult, a small place, and they live all together, living with the scraps, with the very small things which was going out-- going on outside.
And of course, living with the hope that they will survive.
Hope was constantly, constantly with the people.
But I wonder.
I wonder how-- his mother was not a healthy woman-- how they were going on with life in this small hiding place having hope that they will survive.
And they did survive, they did survive.
They survived the war.
His mother knew that I am Jewish.
The parents knew that I am Jewish.
But the people where they were hiding, they didn't know that I am Jewish.
It was a very-- it was on a small cemetery, a Greek Orthodox cemetery.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was one small room, as I recall, where they were living, the Polish people.
And a small kitchen, so small.
And in this kitchen they were living, four people.
How they were able to sleep I don't know, probably staying.
But they, somehow they slept there, they kept Shabbat.
They didn't want to eat meat because it was not kosher.
So even there, in their own way, they kept their Jewishness.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they have-- it was a cemetery constantly close.
If somebody wants to get in, they were alarmed and they knew right away who was going to come.
As I recall, my husband used to help in some Orthodox-- Greek Orthodox died he used to walk and prepared the grave for the-- as a Pole.
This is how I recall this hiding place.
My husband's name?
Yes, I talk about him constantly and not mention the name.
Benjamin [POLISH].
But in the States he remained Benjamin, but from [POLISH] he became Meed.
They said with such a name it would be very difficult in the States to pronounce the name.
So he's Benjamin Meed.
Yes.
Benjamin was involved in the underground.
His particular task was to prepare hiding places for Jews, to make double walls and the Jews can hide under or to make some hiding places where illegal material-- pamphlets or papers-- you can hide.
He was involved.
He also was involved with finding for children places where to hide.
He was involved in his own way, yes.
It was not possible in a normal way.
We have to smuggle them out only when we had a place where to put them, to hide.
And it was much easier with girls than with little boys because little boys were circumcised and little girls can always be as a Polish girl.
There were certain points at the wall of the ghetto where we were able to bribe the police, but they were smuggled-- went on.
But it was not easy.

Sometimes I know a friend of ours, we worked together in the underground and he was later the so-called engineer and made the primitive Molotov cocktails and organized in the ghetto the primitive factory of ammunition.
And Mikhail [POLISH], they smuggled out their 10-month-old daughter and they left the child at the steps of a church and he went away.
And he told me waiting to see what happened with this bundle.
And somebody heard crying and they knocked at the church door.
And they came-- somebody came out and took the baby in, so he knew that the baby is there.
Children were smugglers.
But these were children 10, 11, 12, 13, 14 years old.
And they were the smugglers bringing in some food into the ghetto.
They were small.
And through all kinds of holes, through all kinds of places where they were able and they knew.
They went out, they bought something, brought in.
It was very dangerous.
Many smugglers were killed at the ghetto wall.
Little smugglers.
There were poems, smuggler poems, which writers in the ghetto wrote about the children.
Yes.
If I say-- I said before that I was smuggling in dynamite.
I went back into the ghetto and I went back to the place where it was the center of the illegal youth leadership.
And this was also the place where a primitive ammunition factory of the fighters organization was organized.
And over there I met the leadership.
Quite often I was, always just a smuggler.
Always every smuggler it was dangerous to go over the wall.
And over there I remember when they showed me how they are preparing the so-called Molotov cocktail bottles, which later on it played quite a role during the uprising in the Warsaw ghetto.
I went back, yes, quite a few times.
In this specific place I met Anielewicz, [INAUDIBLE] Anielewicz, who was the leader of the uprising in the Warsaw ghetto, and I met the other people.
It was not a question about going every day or every other day.
When the assignment was-- when I have-- it could have been once a week, once in two weeks.
When it got closer to the uprising, it was more often because it was-- the [INAUDIBLE], the people knew that deportation, that the Warsaw ghetto was going to be liquidated.
We didn't know exactly the date when, but we knew that it will take place.
And at that time, the fighters organization, the groups-- there were about 22 groups-- were already on alert.
And at that time, the communication was a little bit tighter and more often.
The uprising started the 19th of April, 1943.
And I was on the Polish side.
We didn't know exactly when.
And at that time, we heard shooting and then we-- the few people on the Aryan side want to get into the ghetto.
But the ghetto was surrounded more with German patrols and it was impossible to get-- even it was no smuggle, it was nothing.
But I tried and I succeeded in getting in very close to the street which has the ghetto wall in the middle of the street.
One side was in the ghetto and the other side was the Polish side.
There we have one Polish family, [POLISH], who was hiding two Jewish children.
And I went up there.
I didn't bribe the Ukranian who was on guard, but I just told him that I have my mother there and I have to see her and-- and you have to let me through as a Polish girl.
And finally, he let me through.
And at that time, I was staying overnight there.
And I saw how the ghetto was in flames, how the Jewish houses were burning and people were jumping from balcony.
And I remember staying at the window and seeing the flames and Mrs.
[POLISH] constantly telling me, get away from the window, they will see you.
They will see you.
But how could I get away when the world which I was born and connected gets up in flame?
So this was my closeness to the ghetto at the time of the uprising.
Later on, the two people who came into my own apartment, this was [POLISH] and [POLISH], they got out from the ghetto May 8 and they came up.
They knew my address, they knew in the ghetto where I lived.
And they somehow came through the sewers through the ghetto-- from the ghetto [INAUDIBLE] side for help.
It was a hectic time how to get help.
We couldn't organize it right away.
Somehow after a while, we succeeded to organize somebody who walks or knew the sewers for money.
And they also organized two trucks which came to a place near the opening of the sewers.
And this was the place where 60 ghetto fighters later on got out from the ghetto into the woods.
The majority of them got killed in the woods.
A few survived.
OK.
It's enough.
